speaker: Participant 1
text: When I think about the TU Delft campus, the first thing is how impressive it looks, especially around the library and Aula. But it can also feel huge when you are new, like you are walking through a city before you know the shortcuts.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I agree it is impressive, but I did not feel lost for that long. For me the bigger issue was finding a quiet place between lectures, because every good study spot seems taken after lunch.

speaker: Participant 1
text: That is true, but the quiet place problem is part of the size problem for me. If you do not know where smaller buildings are, you just go to the obvious places and then they are full.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Maybe, but I think students also prefer the visible places because they want to bump into friends. So the crowdedness is not only about information, it is also social.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Yes, like the Pulse building feels useful because it is study space and social space at the same time. But then it becomes too loud for people who actually need focus.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Pepper, what do you think would help new students find suitable study places without everyone ending up in the library?

speaker: Pepper
text: Try to take a new student's perspective. What would they need in the first two weeks: a map of quiet spots, signs in buildings, or advice from older students? Compare those options and decide which one would reduce confusion without making the popular places even more crowded.

speaker: Participant 1
text: The new student perspective is useful. I would not trust just a map, because maps do not tell you if a space feels welcoming or if you are allowed to sit there.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I partly disagree. A map with live occupancy would already solve a lot. Feeling welcome matters, but if you know a room is empty, you will at least try it.

speaker: Participant 1
text: But live occupancy could push everyone to the same empty room. Maybe it needs categories, like silent, group work, quick laptop, and social study, not just open seats.

speaker: Participant 2
text: That is fair. Categories would stop people treating every seat as the same. It could also include whether sockets are available, because that decides where I sit.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Exactly, and maybe students could tag spaces after using them. Like this room is quiet before noon but impossible after project deadlines.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I like the tagging idea, although I worry people would not update it. TU Delft apps already ask for things and students ignore notifications.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Then maybe it should not rely on constant ratings. Orientation mentors could give first-year students a starter list, and the app only updates occupancy automatically.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Yes, mentors make it more human. I remember learning more from one older student in my first week than from official pages.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Official information is not wrong, it is just too clean. It does not say that sometimes the best place is a random bench near your faculty because your next lecture is close.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Pepper, what else should we include if the issue is not only finding seats but feeling that the campus belongs to you?

speaker: Pepper
text: What else? Think beyond study rooms: moments of welcome, informal routes, food, signs, student societies, and places to pause. Which of those helps someone feel they are allowed to be on campus, not just passing through it?

speaker: Participant 1
text: Food is a big one. If you can only afford coffee sometimes, you do not hang around after class. You go home or bring food and feel awkward eating it somewhere.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I agree on affordability. But I do not think cheap food alone creates belonging. The faculty culture matters too, because some buildings feel like they are for insiders.

speaker: Participant 1
text: I see that. Architecture has that effect. Some entrances are glass and open, and others feel like you need a reason to enter even if it is public.

speaker: Participant 2
text: The industrial design faculty feels open to me because the projects are visible. Electrical engineering feels more closed, but maybe that is just because I do not study there.

speaker: Participant 1
text: That is exactly the point. If you do not study there, you are unsure if you can use the cafe or printer or just sit. The campus says it is shared, but the buildings do not always say that.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Maybe signs could say open study area for all students. That sounds small, but it removes the social guesswork.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Yes, and maybe not only signs. Screens could show which spaces are open to everyone today, because sometimes rooms are booked and sometimes empty.

speaker: Participant 2
text: That connects back to the map. The map should be both digital and physical, because not everyone wants to open an app when they are already stressed.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Pepper, what is the difference between a wayfinding problem and a belonging problem here?

speaker: Pepper
text: A wayfinding problem is not knowing where to go. A belonging problem is doubting whether you are welcome when you get there. Can you test your ideas against both: does the solution guide students to a place, and does it make permission and social comfort clear?

speaker: Participant 2
text: That distinction helps. A sign solves permission if it says all students welcome. The app solves directions. But social comfort might need actual people or visible norms.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Visible norms could be examples like students from different faculties working there. Maybe posters showing cross-faculty projects would make spaces feel less owned by one group.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I am not sure posters would change behavior. People ignore posters. I think events or shared courses would do more than wall text.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Events are stronger, but they also take organization. Posters could be a low-effort signal. Maybe the deeper solution is programming the spaces with small recurring activities.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Small recurring activities sounds better. Like coffee mornings, project clinics, or language tables in different faculties so you have a reason to enter.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Language tables would help international students. They might also help Dutch students meet people outside their programme, because otherwise groups stay separate.

speaker: Participant 2
text: But there is a risk that only already social students attend. The isolated students may not go to a coffee morning because that is exactly what feels scary.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Then the entry has to be tied to something practical. Not just meet people, but bring your assignment question or find a study partner for a course.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Pepper, if we had to move from brainstorming to choosing, what do we do next?

speaker: Pepper
text: What do we do next? Choose two criteria first. For example, impact on belonging and ease of implementation. Then compare your ideas: study-space map, open-to-all signs, mentor recommendations, and practical social activities. Which option scores well on both criteria?

speaker: Participant 1
text: On impact and ease, open-to-all signs are easy but maybe low impact. Mentor recommendations are medium effort and high impact for first years.

speaker: Participant 2
text: The map is high impact if it works, but implementation is harder because live occupancy needs sensors or booking data. Signs are boring but realistic.

speaker: Participant 1
text: I do not want to dismiss the map, because TU Delft probably has booking data already. Maybe not seat-level data, but room availability at least.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Room availability is not the same as useful study space. A booked room might be empty, and an unbooked room might still feel unavailable if the door is closed.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Good point. So the map is more complex than it sounds. Maybe the first version should not promise live accuracy, just curated study routes.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Curated routes are realistic. Route one could be silent study, route two group work, route three affordable food and short breaks.

speaker: Participant 1
text: That sounds like something mentors could literally walk through during introduction week. Then students remember a route physically, not only digitally.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Yes, and after that it can live as a map. The route has a human introduction first and a digital memory later.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Pepper, what problems could this route idea create and how could we overcome them?

speaker: Pepper
text: Possible problems are outdated information, overcrowding the same routes, and excluding students who miss introduction week. How would you avoid each problem? Consider maintenance, rotating route suggestions, and making the route available through mentors, screens, and the campus app.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Overcrowding is the strongest objection. If every mentor takes students to the same hidden quiet place, it stops being hidden and quiet.

speaker: Participant 1
text: We could have different routes by faculty and time of day. The goal is not one secret place, it is teaching students how to identify suitable places.

speaker: Participant 2
text: That changes the idea. It becomes a skill-building route, not just a list of spaces. I like that more.

speaker: Participant 1
text: And for students who miss introduction week, the app can offer the same route as a fifteen-minute self-guided walk with photos and short notes.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Maintenance could be handled by student assistants each quarter. Not daily updates, just checking whether the route still makes sense.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Quarterly updates are realistic. It also gives student assistants ownership, so the advice remains student-language instead of institutional language.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Pepper, imagine how a commuter student would feel using this idea compared with someone living near campus.

speaker: Pepper
text: Imagine how a commuter student would feel. They may care less about evening hangout spaces and more about reliable places between transport, lectures, and group work. How should your route change so it supports students with limited time on campus?

speaker: Participant 1
text: For commuters, the route should start from bike parking, tram stops, or the train connection, not from the central library. Their campus starts at arrival points.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Yes, and it should show lockers, microwaves, and places where you can sit for forty minutes without buying anything. That is different from a social route.

speaker: Participant 1
text: The no-purchase sitting is important. If belonging depends on buying coffee, then campus is not equally accessible.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I agree, although cafes still matter. Maybe the route labels free pause spaces and paid food spaces separately, so students can choose without guessing.

speaker: Participant 1
text: That is a practical design detail. The route could have icons for free seating, microwave, water refill, quiet, group work, sockets, and accessible entrance.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Accessible entrance should not be an afterthought. Some shortcuts on campus are terrible if you have mobility issues or if it is raining hard.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Pepper, how would that affect whether our idea is accurate or actually useful?

speaker: Pepper
text: It affects usefulness because a route that works only for fast walkers in good weather is not a campus route for everyone. Check accuracy by testing it with different students: commuter, international, mobility-impaired, first-year, and someone carrying project materials. What changes after those tests?

speaker: Participant 2
text: Testing with different students would probably reveal that our route is too idealized. We need versions, not one universal route.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Versions make sense, but too many versions become confusing. Maybe the app asks one question first: what do you need right now?

speaker: Participant 2
text: Then choices could be focus, group work, food break, quick rest, or ask someone. That is simple enough and it respects different needs.

speaker: Participant 1
text: I like that final framing. It is not TU Delft telling students where to go, it is students choosing the kind of campus support they need in that moment.

speaker: Participant 2
text: So our proposal is student-led campus routes: introduced by mentors, maintained by student assistants, available in the app and on screens, with options for different immediate needs.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Yes, and it should include permission signals, not only directions: free seating, open-to-all areas, accessibility, and practical support points.
